1: וַיֵּשֶׁב יַעֲקֹב בְּאֶרֶץ מְגוּרֵי אָבִיו בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן — "Jacob settled in the land of his father's sojournings, the land of Canaan." The verb yāšaḇ (to dwell, settle) — after the Edomite genealogy of ch. 36 (which showed Esau settled in his own territory), the narrator returns to Jacob with the same verb: wayyēšeḇ yaʿăqōḇ. But the contrast is deliberate: Esau settled in his own land (36:8: wayyēšeḇ ʿēśāw bĕhar śēʿîr); Jacob settled in the land of sojournings (mĕgûrîm = temporary resident-alien settlements, the noun from gûr, to sojourn). Esau has his own country; Jacob still sojourns as a covenant-stranger in Canaan.
2: אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב (ʾēlleh tôlĕḏôt yaʿăqōḇ) — "these are the generations of Jacob." The final tôlĕḏôt formula of Genesis; it opens the Joseph narrative. Structurally surprising: the toledoth of Jacob does not narrate Jacob's biography but introduces Joseph, his son. The pattern follows the earlier toledoth of Isaac (25:19ff.) which immediately shifted to Jacob and Esau — the toledoth formula consistently introduces the next generation's story. Naʿar (young man/lad) applied to Joseph at seventeen — the same word used for the servant/lad Ishmael (21:17-18), for the young men who accompanied Abraham (22:3, 5), for Jacob's young sons. At seventeen, Joseph is a naʿar — old enough to work the flocks but young enough to be subordinate. דִּבָּתָם רָעָה (dibbāṯām rāʿāh) — "their bad report" — dibbāh (report, ill report — specifically the kind of report that damages reputation; cf. Num 13:32 where the spies bring back an dibbat hāʾāreṣ, bad report of the land; Prov 10:18 where môṣîʾ dibbāh, one who spreads a bad report, is a fool). Whether Joseph was reporting accurately or slandering his brothers is not resolved; the narrator neither condemns nor defends him.
3: וְיִשְׂרָאֵל אָהַב אֶת-יוֹסֵף מִכָּל-בָּנָיו — "Israel loved Joseph more than all his sons." The name yiśrāʾēl (Israel) is used here rather than Jacob — the narrator uses the covenant name for the patriarch when the narrative touches covenant-line matters. ʾĀhaḇ (the same love-verb used for Isaac's love of Esau, 25:28; for Jacob's love of Rachel, 29:18, 30) — the word for elective, particular love. בֶּן-זְקֻנִים (ben-zĕqûnîm) — "son of old age": the reason for the special love; the same phrase appears in 44:20 (Benjamin is also a ben-zĕqûnîm) — both the Rachel-sons are sons of Jacob's old age. כֻּתֹּנֶת פַּסִּים (kuttōnet passîm) — the famous garment: kuttōnet (tunic, undergarment worn close to the body — the same word as the "skin garments" of 3:21; the priestly tunic of Exod 28:4; 39:27); passîm (difficult: from pas, palm/sole, suggesting a garment reaching to the palms and soles, i.e., a long robe; or "striped"; or "ornamented"). In 2 Sam 13:18-19, the identical phrase kuttōnet passîm describes the garment worn by Tamar and other royal virgin daughters of David — a garment of high status. The robe marks Joseph as the favored heir, which is exactly what the brothers understand it to mean.
4: וַיִּשְׂנְאוּ אֹתוֹ (wayyiśnĕʾû ʾōtô) — "they hated him." Śānēʾ (to hate — the opposite of ʾāhaḇ, to love; the same verb used for Leah, who was hated by Jacob, 29:31-33). The narrative sets up an explicit contrast: Israel loved Joseph (ʾāhab); the brothers hated him (śānēʾ). וְלֹא יָכְלוּ דַּבְּרוֹ לְשָׁלֹם (wĕlōʾ yāḵĕlû dabĕrô lĕšālôm) — "and they were not able to speak to him in peace/shalom." The idiom dāḇar lĕšālôm (to speak to someone's shalom) = to greet with shalom, to speak peacefully and well to someone. The brothers could not give Joseph even the standard greeting-word šālôm. The peace-word that binds covenant community (cf. 29:6: "is he in peace?" hă-šālôm lô?) was impossible between Joseph and his brothers — the relational covenant was already broken.
5: וַיַּחֲלֹם יוֹסֵף חֲלוֹם (wayyaḥălōm yôsēp ḥălôm) — "Joseph dreamed a dream." The verb ḥālam (to dream) + noun ḥălôm (dream) — the cognate accusative construction emphasizes the dreaming: "he dreamed a dream." Dreams as vehicles of divine communication have appeared throughout Genesis (20:3, 6 — Abimelech; 28:12 — Jacob at Bethel; 31:10-11 — Jacob in Paddan-Aram). Joseph's dream-reception establishes him in the line of dream-receivers; his ability to interpret others' dreams (chs. 40-41) develops from this gift. Wayyaggēd lĕʾeḥāyw (he told his brothers) — the telling of the dream is provocative; the text does not tell us Joseph told it naively or strategically; either way, the telling inflamed the situation.
6: שִׁמְעוּ-נָא הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתִּי (šimʿû-nāʾ haḥălôm hazzeh ʾăšer ḥālamtî) — "please listen to this dream that I dreamed." The particle nāʾ (please, I pray — a softening particle used in polite or urgent requests; cf. 12:11; 18:3-4; 22:2) moderates the command; Joseph is asking his brothers to listen. The repetition of the word "dream" in vv. 5-10 — ḥālam and ḥălôm appearing eight times across six verses — saturates the passage with dream-language: this is the dream-story; everything else flows from what the dreams reveal.
7: אֲלֻמָּתִי קָמָה וְגַם-נִצָּבָה (ʾălummāṯî qāmāh wĕgam-niṣṣāḇāh) — "my sheaf rose and stood upright." ʾĂlummāh (sheaf of grain — hapax; appears only here and Ps 126:6 where "carrying sheaves" is the image of joyful return from exile). The sheaf-imagery is harvest/agricultural: Joseph's sheaf rising above the brothers' sheaves is the social reversal in agricultural terms. Qāmāh (rose up — Qal of qûm) + niṣṣāḇāh (stood upright — Niphal of yāṣaḇ, to stand firm/erect); the two verbs together express not just motion (rising) but permanence (standing erect). Wattĕḥaweynāh ʾălummōtêḵem (your sheaves bowed down) — ḥāwāh (to bow down — the same word used for Jacob bowing to Esau in 33:3 and for the patriarchal worship prostrations).
8: הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ עָלֵינוּ (hămālōḵ timlōḵ ʿālênû) — "will you indeed reign over us?" The emphatic infinitive absolute māl ōḵ timlōḵ — the doubling of the root (infinitive absolute + finite verb) expresses intense certainty or indignant disbelief: "are you really going to reign, reign over us?" ʾIm-māšōl timšōl bānû (or will you actually rule us?) — a second emphatic infinitive absolute, using māšal (to rule, have dominion — the same verb as 1:18 where the luminaries were to rule) alongside mālaḵ (to be king/reign). The two parallel rhetorical questions cover both the political verb of kingship (mālaḵ) and the practical verb of dominion (māšal) — the brothers read the dream's meaning accurately. Wayyôsipû ʿôḏ śĕnōʾ ʾōtô (they hated him even more) — the third occurrence of the hate-verb (vv. 4, 5, 8); each chapter-event escalates the brothers' hatred.
9: הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְהַיָּרֵחַ וְאַחַד עָשָׂר כּוֹכָבִים מִשְׁתַּחֲוִים לִי — "the sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me." The cosmological dream replaces the agricultural: agricultural imagery (sheaves) was local; astronomical imagery (sun, moon, stars) is universal. Eleven stars = eleven brothers; the number is exact and immediately transparent. The sun and moon = father and mother; Jacob's interpretation in v. 10 assumes this. Miśtaḥăwîm (bowing down — Hishtaphel/Hitpael of šāḥāh) — the same prostration verb as in v. 7 and used throughout Genesis for worship before God and deference to superiors. The progression from brothers' sheaves bowing (dream 1) to sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing (dream 2) escalates the scope: first the brothers; then the entire family.
10: וַיִּגְעַר-בּוֹ אָבִיו (wayyiḡʿar-bô ʾāḇîw) — "his father rebuked him." Gāʿar (to rebuke, scold — a word for authoritative correction; used for YHWH rebuking the sea, Ps 106:9; for Jesus rebuking the wind, Mark 4:39/parallel). Jacob's rebuke is the appropriate paternal response to what sounds like presumptuous ambition. The question hăḇôʾ nāḇôʾ ʾănî wĕʾimmĕḵā wĕʾaḥêḵā (shall your mother and I and your brothers actually come?) — another emphatic infinitive absolute (bāʾ nāḇôʾ); and the problem: Rachel is already dead (35:19), making the "your mother" reference a narrative tension — either the dream refers to Bilhah (a surrogate mother; some rabbinic interpretation), or the detail is not pressed, or the narrative is not perfectly harmonized at this point. The MT does not resolve it; the narrator records Jacob's interpretation without correcting it.
11: וַיְקַנְאוּ-בוֹ אֶחָיו (wayyĕqannĕʾû-ḇô ʾeḥāyw) — "his brothers were jealous of him." Qinnēʾ (Piel of qānāʾ, to be jealous, zealous — the root of qinʾāh, jealousy; God's jealousy for his people uses this root: Exod 20:5; 34:14; the same capacity that drives covenant-protectiveness in God drives destructive rivalry in humans). Wĕʾāḇîw šāmar ʾet-haddāḇār (but his father kept the matter in mind) — šāmar (to keep, observe, guard — used for keeping covenant, keeping the law; here: keeping the matter in mind, pondering it). The contrast: brothers jealous; father keeps/ponders. Luke 2:19 uses identical idiom for Mary pondering the shepherds' words (syneterein en tē kardia) — the keeping-in-mind of a mystery not yet understood.
12: וַיֵּלְכוּ אֶחָיו לִרְעוֹת אֶת-צֹאן אֲבִיהֶם בִּשְׁכֶם — "his brothers went to pasture their father's flock near Shechem." Shechem is the location of the Dinah/Simeon-Levi massacre (ch. 34); Jacob's sons have returned to the site of their earlier violence. The narrative moves forward without explicit connection to ch. 34, but the reader carries the knowledge that Shechem is a dangerous location in this family's history. The brothers' presence there sets the stage for the mission Jacob gives Joseph.
13: הִנֵּנִי (hinnēnî, here I am) — Joseph's response to his father's call. The covenant-readiness formula of Abraham (hinnēnî in 22:1, 7, 11) and Isaac (22:7) is now on Joseph's lips when his father calls him to go toward danger. Joseph does not know what awaits him; he simply makes himself available. The structural echo of Abraham's hinnēnî at the Akedah is not accidental: Joseph, like Isaac, will be sent on a journey by his father that involves figurative death and return. The word is one of the richest single words in Genesis for its structural weight.
14: לֵךְ-נָא רְאֵה אֶת-שְׁלוֹם אַחֶיךָ וְאֶת-שְׁלוֹם הַצֹּאן — "go, please, see the peace/welfare of your brothers and the peace of the flock." The word šālôm (peace, well-being, wholeness) appears twice in Jacob's commission: check on your brothers' šālôm; check on the flock's šālôm. The brothers who "could not speak his shalom" (v. 4) are the ones Joseph is sent to check on. The šālôm-word connects: Jacob sends Joseph to seek what the brothers refused to give. Mēʿēmeq ḥeḇrôn (from the Valley of Hebron) — Hebron is where Abraham was buried (25:9-10) and where Isaac lived (35:27); the patriarchal heartland sends Joseph out.
15: וַיִּמְצָאֵהוּ אִישׁ (wayyimṣāʾēhû ʾîš) — "a man found him." The ʾîš (man) who appears to the wandering Joseph is unnamed and unexplained — he appears, gives directions, and disappears. The narrative economy of his appearance is striking: without this nameless man, Joseph would not have found his brothers at Dothan. Some read the ʾîš as an angelic figure (consistent with the pattern of unnamed divine messengers in Genesis); the text neither confirms nor denies. Tōʿeh baśśāḏeh (wandering in the field) — the verb tāʿāh (to wander, go astray — used for both physical and moral wandering; cf. Ps 119:176: "I have wandered like a lost sheep"; Isa 53:6: "we all have wandered"). Joseph is literally lost; the man's intervention is providential.
16: אֶת-אַחַי אָנֹכִי מְבַקֵּשׁ (ʾet-ʾaḥay ʾānōḵî mĕḇaqqēš) — "my brothers I am seeking." The emphatic pronoun ʾānōḵî (I — the full first-person form used for emphasis) + the Piel participle mĕḇaqqēš (seeking, searching) — Joseph's search for his brothers is expressed with intensity. The Piel of bāqaš (to seek) is the word for intentional seeking: seeking God (Isa 55:6; Ps 27:8), seeking someone with purpose. Joseph's mission is to seek his brothers; the terrible irony is that he finds them and they destroy him.
17: נָסְעוּ מִזֶּה (nāsĕʿû mizzeh, they moved on from here) — the brothers have moved from Shechem to Dothan. Nāsaʿ (to journey, set out, pull stakes — the verb of the Wilderness journeying: the Israelites "set out" from each campsite in Num 33). Dōṯānāh (Dothan) — a real location north of Shechem (modern Tell Dothan); identified with Dothan in the plain; its remoteness from Jacob at Hebron gives the brothers time and isolation for what follows. Wayyāḇōʾ yôsēp ʾaḥărê ʾeḥāyw (Joseph went after his brothers) — ʾaḥărê (after, behind) — Joseph follows his brothers; the word subtly echoes the narrative theme of following versus leading that the dreams have set up.
18: וַיִּרְאוּ אֹתוֹ מֵרָחֹק (wayyirʾû ʾōtô mērāḥōq) — "they saw him from a distance." Rāḥōq (far off, at a distance) — the brothers see Joseph before he reaches them; the distance gives them time to conspire. Wĕyitnakkĕlû ʾōtô (they conspired against him) — nākal (Hitpael, to conspire, plot with deceit against someone — a rare and strong word for treacherous plotting; cf. Ps 105:25 where Egypt "conspired" against Israel; Mal 1:14 where it is used for deceptive sacrifice). The plotting is premeditated and deceitful; the Hitpael form expresses the reflexive-intensive: they strategized with each other against him.
19: הִנֵּה בַּעַל הַחֲלֹמוֹת הַלָּזֶה בָּא (hinnēh baʿal haḥălōmôt hallāzeh bāʾ) — "here comes this master of dreams!" Baʿal haḥălōmôt (master/owner of the dreams — baʿal = lord, master, owner, husband; the term of possession or mastery). The phrase is mocking: "this dream-master" — a derisive title that simultaneously acknowledges the power of the dreams (they are real dreams) and expresses contempt for the dreamer. Hallāzeh (this one, this person here — a demonstrative pronoun with a distancing, sometimes contemptuous force: "this one there"). The sarcasm is exact Hebrew: baʿal haḥălōmôt hallāzeh = "this dream-lord here."
20: וְנִרְאֶה מַה-יִּהְיוּ חֲלֹמֹתָיו (wĕnirʾeh mah-yihyû ḥălōmōṯāyw) — "and we shall see what becomes of his dreams." The bitter irony of the verse: the brothers' plan to kill the dreamer and eliminate the dreams will in fact set the dreams' fulfillment in motion. The cistern they throw him into will lead to Egypt; Egypt will lead to Pharaoh's dreams; Pharaoh's dreams will lead to Joseph's rule; Joseph's rule will bring the brothers to bow. Ûnirʾeh (and we shall see) — the Niphal cohortative "we will see" — the brothers intend to see the dreams fail; what they will see, in ch. 42-44, is the dreams come true. The narrative's deepest irony runs through this single clause.
21: וַיִּשְׁמַע רְאוּבֵן וַיַּצִּלֵהוּ מִיָּדָם (wayyišmaʿ rĕʾûḇēn wayyaṣṣîlēhû miyyāḏām) — "Reuben heard and rescued him from their hands." Šāmaʿ (to hear, listen — the foundational verb of response and covenant-loyalty; cf. the Šĕmaʿ: "Hear, O Israel"). Reuben's hearing leads to intervention: hiṣṣîl (Hiphil of nāṣal, to rescue, deliver — the same word used for God's deliverance: Exod 3:8; Ps 50:15). Reuben uses the rescue-word that belongs to divine action. ʾAl-tišpĕḵû-dām (do not shed blood) — šāpaḵ dām (to shed blood) — the same blood-shedding language of Gen 9:6 (the Noahic covenant against bloodshed) and of the later Mosaic law against murder. Reuben invokes the foundational prohibition.
22: לְמַעַן הַצִּיל אֹתוֹ מִיָּדָם לַהֲשִׁיבוֹ אֶל-אָבִיו (lĕmaʿan haṣṣîl ʾōtô miyyāḏām lăhăšîḇô ʾel-ʾāḇîw) — "to rescue him from their hands and return him to his father." The narrator reveals Reuben's interior motive — an unusual instance of explicit motive-disclosure in Genesis narrative (cf. the same transparency in 37:33 when Jacob interprets the robe, and in 31:31 when Jacob explains his flight). Reuben's plan: use the cistern as a delaying tactic; when the others' attention is elsewhere, retrieve Joseph and return him to Jacob. The plan fails because Reuben is absent when the Ishmaelite traders arrive (v. 29). The narrative does not tell us where Reuben went; his absence at the crucial moment is unexplained.
23: וַיַּפְשִׁיטוּ אֶת-יוֹסֵף אֶת-כֻּתָּנְתּוֹ אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים — "they stripped Joseph of his robe, the ornamented robe he was wearing." Pāšaṭ (Hiphil: to strip, take off clothing from someone — the word used for removing priestly garments in Lev 6:4; Num 20:26; here it is violent removal of the status-garment). The repetition: "his robe" + "the ornamented robe" — the double identification of the ketonet passim at the moment of its removal emphasizes what is being taken: not just a garment but the father's love, the heir-sign, the status marker. The robe that Jacob made for Joseph is stripped off; the garment will be soaked in blood and used to deceive Jacob (v. 31-33) — the robe becomes the instrument of the deception of the father by the sons, mirroring Jacob's deception of Isaac with the goatskin.
24: וַיַּשְׁלִכוּ אֹתוֹ הַבֹּרָה (wayyašlîḵû ʾōtô habbōrāh) — "they threw him into the pit." Šālaḵ (to throw, cast — the violent disposal verb; used for the discarding of the golden calf into water, Exod 32:24; for the casting of Jonah into the sea, Jonah 1:15; for judgment-casting in Jer 22:19). Bôr (pit, cistern — the word covers dry storage cisterns, wells, and metaphorically the underworld/Sheol; Ps 28:1; 30:4 [Heb 30:4]; Isa 14:15; 38:18 use bôr for the realm of death). Joseph enters a bôr — a death-space; the narrator adds: wĕhabbôr rēq ʾên bô māyim (the pit was empty; there was no water in it). The absence of water (ʾên māyim) prevents immediate death by drowning but leaves Joseph without resources — alive but helpless. The emptiness of the pit is the condition of his survival.
25: וַיֵּשְׁבוּ לֶאֱכָל-לֶחֶם (wayyēšĕḇû lĕʾĕḵol-leḥem) — "they sat down to eat bread." The shocking juxtaposition: Joseph is in the pit; the brothers sit to eat. The eating-while-the-other-suffers will be remembered in the later narrative — Judah's speech to Joseph in 44:18-34 does not mention the pit, but the brothers' guilty memory of "when they saw the distress of his soul, when he pleaded with us, and we did not listen" (42:21) suggests the eating scene was remembered as the moment of hardness. ʾŌrĕḥat yišmĕʿēʾlîm (a caravan of Ishmaelites) — ʾōrāḥāh (caravan — the camel-train of long-distance traders; Ps 68:10 uses the word for the "company" of God's people). The Ishmaelites are from Gilead, carrying nĕḵōʾt ûṣĕrî wālōṭ (spices, balm, and myrrh) — three aromatic trade goods; the balm of Gilead (ṣĕrî) appears in Jer 8:22; 46:11 as the therapeutic product of the Gilead region. The trading route from Gilead to Egypt is historically attested.
26: מַה-בֶּצַע כִּי נַהֲרֹג אֶת-אָחִינוּ (mah-beṣaʿ kî nahărōg ʾet-ʾāḥînû) — "what profit is there if we kill our brother?" Beṣaʿ (gain, profit — frequently with the connotation of dishonest or unjust profit; cf. Exod 18:21; 1 Sam 8:3; Isa 56:11; Jer 6:13 where bōṣēaʿ beṣaʿ, "greedy for gain," characterizes the corrupt). Judah's argument is pragmatic-economic, not moral: the question is not "is it wrong to kill Joseph?" but "what do we gain?" Wĕkissînû ʾet-dāmô (and we cover up his blood) — kāsāh dām (to cover blood) — the idiom for concealing a murder; cf. Job 16:18 where Job asks the earth not to cover his blood (i.e., not to conceal the injustice done to him). Judah's argument: death is unproductive and creates a bloodguilt that must be hidden.
27: כִּי-אָחִינוּ בְשָׂרֵנוּ הוּא (kî-ʾāḥînû ḇĕśārēnû hûʾ) — "for he is our brother, our flesh." Bāśār (flesh — the word for kinship solidarity: "bone of my bone and flesh of my flesh" [2:23]; the kinship-solidarity principle that Judah invokes: you do not harm your own flesh). The argument: sell him rather than kill him — he remains alive, we gain silver, and we have not violated the flesh-solidarity obligation. Wayyišmĕʿû ʾeḥāyw (his brothers listened) — the verb šāmaʿ (to hear, listen, obey); the brothers consent to Judah's proposal. The same verb that opened Reuben's intervention (v. 21: "Reuben heard") closes Judah's: both brothers use the hearing-verb, but with very different outcomes.
28: אַנְשֵׁי מִדְיָן סֹחֲרִים (ʾanšê miḏyān sōḥărîm) — "Midianite traders." The buyers are variously called Ishmaelites (vv. 25, 27) and Midianites (v. 28) and Medanites (v. 36). The three names likely reflect the overlapping tribal confederacies of the Transjordan: Ishmaelites and Midianites were closely related (both descended from Abraham — through Hagar and Keturah respectively, 25:2; 16:15) and shared the same trade routes. The narrative uses them interchangeably as desert traders. בְּעֶשְׂרִים כָּסֶף (bĕʿeśrîm kāsep, for twenty pieces of silver) — the price; Lev 27:5 sets the redemption price for persons aged 5-20 at twenty shekels — a standard slave-price for a young person in ANE contexts. The silver that Joseph's brothers receive for him will reappear in the narrative: 42:25-35 (brothers' silver returned in their sacks); 44:2 (Joseph's silver cup planted in Benjamin's sack). The silver motif runs through the Joseph story.
29: וַיָּשָׁב רְאוּבֵן אֶל-הַבּוֹר (wayyāšāḇ rĕʾûḇēn ʾel-habbôr) — "Reuben returned to the pit." The verb šûḇ (to return) — Reuben comes back to execute his rescue plan; he finds Joseph gone. Wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bĕḡāḏāyw (he tore his clothes) — the garment-tearing of mourning; the same gesture Jacob will make in v. 34. Reuben tears his clothes at the empty pit just as Jacob will tear his clothes at the blood-stained robe. Both mournings are real grief; both are also responses to deception. Reuben's grief is the grief of a failed rescuer; the narrative records it without condemnation but also without full absolution — Reuben did not prevent the sale.
30: הַיֶּלֶד אֵינֶנּוּ וַאֲנִי אָנָה אֲנִי-בָא (hayyeleḏ ʾênennû waʾănî ʾānāh ʾănî-ḇāʾ) — "the boy is gone! Where can I go?" Yeleḏ (child, boy — a more intimate word than naʿar; used for newborns and young children; Reuben calls Joseph a child, the vulnerable one). ʾĀnāh ʾănî-ḇāʾ (where can I go?) — the question of desperate accountability: "I, where am I to go?" — meaning "I am responsible; there is nowhere to hide from this failure; how do I face my father?" The question anticipates Jacob's grief: Jacob will also have "nowhere to go" in his mourning (v. 35: he refuses to be comforted). The two questions — Reuben's "where do I go?" and Jacob's "I will go to Sheol" — frame the family's devastation.
31: וַיִּשְׁחֲטוּ שְׂעִיר עִזִּים (wayyišḥăṭû śĕʿîr ʿizzîm) — "they slaughtered a male goat." Šāḥaṭ (to slaughter — the technical verb for ritual slaughter of animals; used for sacrificial killing throughout Leviticus and Numbers). The brothers use the slaughter-verb — the same action Joseph's sons Ephraim and Manasseh will perform in Egypt. A male goat (śĕʿîr ʿizzîm) — śĕʿîr (hairy goat; the word is identical to the territory Seir, Esau's land; and to Esau himself who was hairy). Jacob was deceived by his sons Simeon and Levi (and the Dinah-revenge) into eating goat in his old age; now he will be deceived by the goat's blood into believing Joseph is dead. The family's goat-deception motif runs from 27:9 (goat for the Esau-blessing deception) to here.
32: שְׁלַח אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים (šālaḥ ʾet-kĕtōnet happasîm) — "they sent the ornamented robe." Šālaḥ (to send — the pivotal action-verb of the narrative: Jacob sent Joseph [v. 13]; God will send Joseph to Egypt ahead of the family [45:5, 7-9]; now the brothers "send" the bloody robe). Hakkēr-nāʾ (please examine/recognize) — nākar (Hiphil: to recognize, examine closely — Tamar will use the same word in 38:25-26 when she sends Judah's identifying objects: "please recognize whose these are"). Hakkēr-nāʾ is technically a request; the brothers' usage is a demand disguised as a request. The robe that Jacob gave Joseph now comes back covered in blood; the recognition is forced on Jacob.
33: וַיַּכִּרָהּ (wayyakkirāh) — "he recognized it." The same verb as v. 32 (nākar); Jacob performs the recognition the brothers demanded. Kĕtōnet bĕnî ḥayyāh rāʿāh ʾăḵālatĕhû (my son's robe! A ferocious animal has devoured him) — Jacob's interpretation is exactly what the brothers intended him to conclude. Ṭārōp ṭōrap yôsēp (Joseph has surely been torn to pieces) — emphatic infinitive absolute: ṭārap (to tear prey, as a predator tears its victim — used for the tearing of the shepherd's flock in 31:39 where Jacob argued he bore the losses of torn animals; now it is turned against Jacob's own son). The narrative has Jacob use the same ṭerepāh vocabulary he used in his legal defense of Laban: there he claimed torn animals were his responsibility; here his son is the one said to be torn.
34: וַיִּקְרַע יַעֲקֹב שִׂמְלֹתָיו וַיָּשֶׂם שַׂק בְּמָתְנָיו — "Jacob tore his garments and put on sackcloth on his waist." Qāraʿ (to tear — the standard mourning gesture; the garment torn at the neck or chest; performed for death of a first-degree relative: 2 Sam 1:11-12; 13:31; Job 1:20). Śaq (sackcloth — the coarse material of mourning and fasting; cf. 1 Kgs 21:27; Joel 1:8; Isa 58:5; Jon 3:5-6). Wayyitʾabbēl ʿal-bĕnô yāmîm rabbîm (he mourned for his son many days) — ʾāḇal (to mourn — the formal mourning posture); yāmîm rabbîm (many days — an indefinite period of extended mourning; 22 years is the implied duration, since Joseph is 30 when he stands before Pharaoh [41:46] and was 17 when sold).
35: וַיְמָאֵן לְהִתְנַחֵם (waymāʾēn lĕhitnáḥem) — "he refused to be comforted." Māʾēn (Piel: to refuse, reject — the same verb for Pharaoh refusing to let Israel go, Exod 7:14; 8:2; for the refusal of covenant terms). Jacob refuses consolation: not merely unable to be comforted but actively refusing it. Hitnáḥem (Hitpael of nāḥam: to be comforted, to console oneself — the reflexive of the comfort-root; Ps 119:52 uses the same Hitpael for "I comfort myself with your ordinances"). כִּי-אֵרֵד אֶל-בְּנִי אָבֵל שְׁאֹלָה (for I will go down to Sheol mourning for my son) — šĕʾôl (Sheol — the underworld, the realm of the dead in OT cosmology; a dark, inert place of no return; cf. Ps 6:5; 30:3; Eccl 9:10). Jacob will mourn until he joins Joseph in death: the mourning and the grave are inseparable for him. The tragic irony: Joseph is alive. Jacob mourns for 22 years for a living son.
36: וְהַמְּדָנִים מָכְרוּ אֹתוֹ אֶל-מִצְרָיִם (wĕhammĕḏānîm māḵrû ʾōtô ʾel-miṣrāyim) — "the Medanites sold him into Egypt." Mĕḏānîm (Medanites — yet a third name for the traders, from Medan son of Abraham and Keturah, 25:2) — the third name-variant in four verses (Ishmaelites, Midianites, Medanites) reflects the fluid tribal identity of the region; the narrative' s interchangeable use of these names is consistent with the actual kinship connections between these groups. לְפוֹטִיפַר סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה (lĕpôṭîpar sĕrîs parʿōh) — "to Potiphar, an official of Pharaoh." Sārîs (official, courtier — from Akkadian ša-rēši, "of the head/of the court"; can mean eunuch but here likely means royal official, given Potiphar's married status in ch. 39). Śar haṭṭabbāḥîm (captain of the guard) — literally "chief of the slaughterers/executioners"; ṭabbāḥ (butcher, cook, executioner/guard — a royal official with judicial and military functions in the ANE court; the Akkadian cognate ṭabbāḥu designates a similar court functionary). Joseph enters the most powerful empire of the ancient world as a slave — the lowest possible position — in the service of a man whose title is "chief of executioners." The descent from favorite son to condemned slave is complete.
